speaker: interviewer
text: The date is April 9, 1996. Survivor and war crimes trials participant is Lawrence Rhee. His last name at birth was Rhee. His last name now is Rhee. The interviewer is Louise Bobrow. The city is Albuquerque in the state of New Mexico. The country is the United States of America, and the language is in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Louise Bobrow. Today's date is April 9, 1996. I am conducting an interview with survivor and participant in the war crimes trials, with Lawrence Rhee. The interview is being conducted in Albuquerque, New Mexico, in the United States of America. Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your complete name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lawrence L. Rhee. R-H-E-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-A-W-R-E-N-C-E. Middle initial, L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does the L stand for--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's just an initial.

speaker: interviewer
text: --anything? Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Lothar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. L-O-T-H-A-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your first name. But Rhee, the last name--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was the same. And the middle initial remained just L.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Everybody calls me Larry. That's the only thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your present day age?

speaker: interviewee
text: In July, I'll be 78.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Recklinghausen, Germany. R-E-C-K-L-I-N-G-H-A-U-S-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe where in Germany this town was located?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in the province of Westphalia, near the Dutch-Belgium border in the Rhine-Ruhr Valley, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- in the Rhine-Ruhr Valley, roughly speaking, 125 miles or so north of the city of Cologne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has your family lived there for many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My family on my father's side has been traced back to before 1800. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on my mother's side, even further, like 1750.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up in-- do you say-- Recklinghausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Recklinghausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall the approximate size, the population?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, Recklinghausen had about 80,000 inhabitants.

speaker: interviewer
text: And out of the 80,000 inhabitants, how many inhabitants were Jewish? Do you have any idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very well. No, I don't recall. But there were quite a number. I mean, it was not-- we didn't have a Jewish community where lots of Jews lived together. We had our synagogue, and I remember there were some Orthodox Jews. But to the best of my knowledge, there were no Reform Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe Recklinghausen? What kind of town was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an industrial town, you could call it. We had coal mines there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was about the only industry that I can think of that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Jews that lived in Recklinghausen, what types of occupations, mainly, were they involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly merchants, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] doctors, lawyers. I remember a few. And yeah, a pretty good mix.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall your home or apartment, the house that you lived in in Recklinghausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the street and the number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [GERMAN] 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a private home or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was an apartment. It was above a store. The store was on the bottom, and our apartments were on top.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you describe the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice, cozy-- pleasant living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have many rooms, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess we-- see, we actually lived on two floors, the floor immediately above the store, and then another floor above that, where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the town where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moritz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rhee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a merchant.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of merchant?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of those little general stores. No groceries, but toys and crystalware and little bit of hardware, porcelain.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way do you mean? In--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, what do you recall of him? Before you left Germany, what was he like? Do you have memories of--

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was, as I say, a merchant. He took care of the business. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a good father. Strict, yes. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very-- very easy to get along with, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memories of anything you might have done special with him? Can you recall anything? As a little boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go fishing some on the weekends, nearby rivers. And that was our weekend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Else. E-L-S-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seligmann. S-E-L-I-G-M-A-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have an occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that during the war, she became a nurse's aide to take care of the German soldiers. But she actually was a housewife. That was her main occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which war are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: World War I.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very kind and good person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any special memories you can recollect of anything you did with her as a youngster?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing special. She took care of the family and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have sisters and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still living is one brother. He is a veteran of Bataan, the March of Death, and is retired in Mexico. He went to live in Mexico.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heinz L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he younger or older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Older, he's older. And I have two sisters deceased.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anita, and the other one was Ursula.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Both of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their married names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anita was Colby, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and Ursula was Johnson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe any memories or recollections, when you were younger, as siblings, anything you did specially together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We played together, fought together, [LAUGHS] like all children do. And but generally we got along very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters were younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one, my grandmother on my mother's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Bertha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bertha. And her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-R-T-H-A. Seligmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a wonderful person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: When she grew too old to handle her own affairs, yes, she lived with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't know your other grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't know them at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you mentioned earlier that your family history went way back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything of unusual interest of your family since they went way back in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my name is derived from the little town of Rheda, R-H-E-D-A, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Westphalia, also, where my forebears lived. I don't know how many generations lived there. But up to that particular time, around 1806 or so, the Jews were only permitted to have their Jewish names. But my forebears got around that by calling them Rhe, so the people would know from where they came-- R-H-E. And in those days, we spelled our name with an accent aigu over the first E and the L and the E. That's where the double E comes in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- for four years, I went to a Jewish parochial school in Recklinghausen where we had eight grades all in one room, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I studied Hebrew, Bible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then my father-- well, my parents both were not-- let's call it overly religious. But we were Jewish and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall how some of the Jewish holidays were celebrated in your home? For example, did you celebrate Shabbat on Friday evenings?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went to the parochial school, I learned to celebrate it and also started to observe Yom Kippur and Rosh Hashana and other-- Purim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do any holidays stand out in your mind that were celebrated in your home? For example, Passover-- do you have any memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we never had Passover in our home. We were invited many times, but we never had one in our home that I could recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall anything about the Passover celebrated in other homes? Does anything stand out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were the regular Passovers, and I enjoyed them. We enjoyed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you usually went to other relatives for--

speaker: interviewee
text: To friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that-- where did that take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the synagogue in Recklinghausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what can you recall about your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to study for it very hard and I had to memorize my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] call it a speech. My-- and it was not a very big affair, fairly small, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish community of Recklinghausen, were there are many synagogues in the town? Approximately how many synagogues were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only-- only one that I know. And I know the Orthodox people met in somebody's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other organizations or associations, Jewish theater or a Jewish newspaper that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was the relationship of the Jewish population to the non-Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, all I can talk about is my own personal experience, and we got along fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned schooling. You went mainly to a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: For four years, a grammar school. And then I went to high school for six years. But I couldn't finish because of Hitler at that time already.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe yourself as a youngster? What were you like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how to d-- [CHUCKLES] I think I was fairly easygoing. I had my circle of friends, I had my due fights [LAUGHS] with the neighbor kids, you know. But generally speaking--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for fun and entertainment with your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We played together quite a bit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We sometimes went to other-- to the other people's home, or they came to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in any sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I joined a gymnastic club when I was about eight or nine years old. And then after Hitler came to power, I was kicked out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was about 16 or 17 years old, and then I was kicked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you good in gymnastics?

speaker: interviewee
text: In some parts of it I was, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall anything at all about the Olympics that were held in 1936?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wasn't there then. You see, I left Germany in December 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had left already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your dreams and ambitions of the future when you were a youngster?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to become a doctor, a medical doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many non-Jewish friends in Recklinghausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know, classmates. And my parents had quite a group of non-Jewish friends, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you personally experience any anti-Semitism prior to Hitler's rise of power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I recall, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What changes did you observe after Hitler rose to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: For one thing, I-- my best friend in school, my classmate, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] could no longer speak to me. His father was a Social Democrat. And he wound up in a concentration camp in the early-- during the early part. He was released about six months after. But he had been thoroughly brainwashed, and his son could no longer speak to me. His parents would not permit me to have any contact at all, for fear that it might harm his dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other changes started happening that you observed personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for one, I remember one thing. It's not a change so much, but a happening. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I was in high school, two of my classmates, with whom I got along very well, by the way, had held minor leadership positions in the Hitler Youth. And suddenly, they were taken out of school at about-- for about two or three weeks. I forget the exact time. And then they-- when they came back, they told me confidentially that they had been put into military camp for learning to shoot pistols, rifles, and machine guns at the age of about 16. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The teachers, by the way, that I had did not show any anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else occur to you personally? Any other changes? For example, in school, were you treated any differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, as I said, the kids treated me fairly well. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got along fine. And the teachers, as I mentioned already-- they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] didn't show any particular anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, in the earliest years, now, ever see Hitler personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear some of his speeches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I heard many of his speeches.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here's the thing. You see, Germany had just lived through terrible inflation. [COUGHS] Excuse me. And all of us thought that once Hitler could stabilize the economical situation that they would-- that he would relent on his anti-Semitism, but that didn't happen, of course. And talking about inflation, I remember one particular incident. In fact, I still have the coin. Around 1923, my brother asked my mother to give him some money to buy a lollipop. And my mother gave him 5 million marks. And he came back crying from the grocery store across the street. He couldn't buy a lollipop for 5 million marks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would that have been the equivalent to in dollars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if it were nowadays, it would be $1,400,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in those days--

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't buy a lollipop for it. The mark-- German money was worthless. A shirt, I read someplace, cost-- I don't-- millions and millions of marks, a plain old shirt. And I'm told-- now, I didn't see this, or at least I don't recall it-- that people went and got their wages, got their payment, and went to the banks [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] having wheat barrels to hold their money. There was so much paper, there was no other way to carry it. But I don't recall that. I have been told this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your neighbors change their attitudes toward you? Did you begin to see any changes as Hitler rose to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. We-- you know, we had grown up together and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had been playing together. Not very much, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned you were thrown out of school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not thrown out. I couldn't continue. Let's put-- after a certain year, after what we would call 10th grade here, which is a certain cut-off point in Germany, was a certain kind of cut-off point in Germany then. I could go no longer. It wouldn't have been feasible anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that occur? Did they send a notice? Or did--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We just didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: They told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Re-enroll. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you at the end of the year that you could not come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My parents were informed that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you could no longer go back to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to work on a farm. It was a setup where you were, like, going to school, a farm school. But these were Jewish owners. Not in Recklinghausen. I worked in two places. One was in Silesia, and the other one near Berlin. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were taught a little theory in farming and also had to do the hard practical work that goes with it, like plowing, hoeing weeds. And you mention it, and we had to do it-- threshing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked on the farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Milking cows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other jobs at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Oh, when I was a kid, I had a little vacation job here, there, trying to earn a little pocket money, but no job, as such, to live on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances that you decided with your family to leave Germany? How did that occur?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very fortunate. My mother had three brothers who had left Germany before the turn of the century. In fact, the first one came over in 1890 or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: To where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Bernalillo, New Mexico. Bernalillo's about 20 miles north from-- of Albuquerque. And they, of course, knew much more what was going on in Germany, due to the news media than we in Germany knew. And they supplied us with the affidavits to be able to get the whole family into the United States. My mother was-- my mother's trip was delayed by about a year and a half or two because she was in Switzerland. She had undergone a lung operation. But she was in Switzerland at the time, and-- but she joined us later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the papers that you needed that were necessary to go to the United States were affidavits. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you need any sort of visa? Visas?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess. It was so long ago, I-- my-- I had some relatives in Berlin, also a brother of my mother, who did all the paperwork because the consulate was there, and everything was-- all I know is that we only went to the consulate once and were interrogated a little bit, and then we got our papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what kinds of questions they asked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that I didn't know where Bernalillo was. [LAUGHS] No idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it actually take until you could leave Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how long the process took. But there were quotas already. But it took a little time, though. But in-- we left in December 1935, spent Christmas and New Year on the high seas. We went through the Panama Canal and landed in Los Angeles. That's the part of the trip I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the ship that you were on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The Elbe, the German river, Elbe, was the name of the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you, leaving the town of Recklinghausen, the town where you were born in. Can you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, I really, at that time, was already on the farm. So I had no particular-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wasn't homesick or anything. My dad, though, I remember, cried when he was aboard the ship. But us kids didn't have that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that particular [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] feeling about it.
